judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with comparable rigor; Report 2 edges ahead with runtime verification of the seed-tenant behavior and an added check on job/message-client realism, while Report 1 adds a note on missing test coverage — overall comparable quality.

report_1.evidence: Reminder messages **will be sent automatically**. The decision is made in `backend/app/feature_flags.py`, function `auto_send_reminders_enabled`, at **line 11**... if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None: # line 11 ... return True # line 12

report_2.evidence:
Reminders **will be sent automatically**... The deciding code is `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py`, lines 11–12:
```python
if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
    return True
```

report_1.evidence: I would change it at `backend/app/feature_flags.py:11–13` so an unset value returns **False** — e.g. `return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))` alone (which yields `False` for missing/None/falsey), making enrollment strictly opt-in.

report_2.evidence: For a feature that contacts real patients (cost, consent/compliance, and trust implications), the safe and expected default is **opt-in (off)**. I would change `backend/app/feature_flags.py:12` from `return True` to `return False`.

report_1.evidence: `git show 25509a7` (SLOT-190) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the `if ... is None: return True` grandfather-on pattern for a UI display preference. `git show aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214) added `auto_send_reminders_enabled` as a byte-for-byte structural clone of that resolver... Grandfather-on is appropriate for the calendar view (a convenience-only UI preference where being wrong is low-cost), but the two flags are in different risk categories, and the null-default must be re-decided per flag rather than inherited.

report_2.evidence: It was **copied from the pre-existing calendar-view flag**. `git show 25509a7` (SLOT-190) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the `if ... is None: return True` pattern for a purely cosmetic UI preference... Defaulting to on is defensible for a cosmetic view preference, where being wrong is harmless and reversible. It is **not** a sound basis for the reminder toggle: the semantics are entirely different (a local display preference vs. sending messages to patients).

report_1.evidence: The grep confirms there is *no* master switch / kill-switch anywhere — the job is gated solely by the per-tenant value. A per-tenant outbound flag should be ANDed with an env-level master so the whole feature can be turned on/off per deploy and there's a single rollback affordance.

report_2.evidence: **Add a global gate / staged rollout / kill-switch.** There is no env var or global flag anywhere (`grep` found none); the only control is the per-tenant toggle. Add a way to dark-launch and instantly disable the whole job without a code deploy.
